revision.comment: Created page with "Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it. Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines. Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial..."
revision.text:
Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Related pages ==
* [[Myojin Taisha]]
== Sources == [[Category:Categorization of Shinto Shrines]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Shikiwaisha]] to [[Shikigaisha]]
revision.text:
Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Related pages ==
* [[Myojin Taisha]]
== Sources == [[Category:Categorization of Shinto Shrines]]

Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Related pages ==
* [[Myojin Taisha]]
== Sources == [[Category:Categorization of Shinto Shrines]] [[ja:式外社]]

revision.comment: importing simple
revision.text:
Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>

revision.text:
Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shikigeisha]][[ja:式外社]][[simple:Shikigeisha]][[zh:式外社]] Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}} which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Related pages ==
* [[:en:Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]]
== References == {{reflist}}
}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]]

revision.text:
Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>

revision.text:
Shikigeisha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>

[[Category:shikigesha]]

Shikigesha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigesha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigesha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigesha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigesha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigesha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>

Shikigeisha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigeisha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigeisha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigeisha contrast with {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigeisha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
== Related pages ==

Shikigesha (式外社) refers to [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] that were known to have existed in the early 10th century when the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho]] was being written, but were not included in it.
Shikigesha, therefore, were considered "off-register" or "unofficial" shrines that were not recognized by the government as official state shrines.
Shikigesha can be further classified into various categories, including shrines outside the control of the imperial court, those with their own power and influence, shrines that integrated Buddhism into their practices, and shrines managed by Buddhist monks. Additionally, some Shikigesha lacked proper formal shrine buildings.
Shikigesha contrast with [[Shikinaisha]] which are shrines that were recorded in the Engishiki.
[[Kokushi genzaisha]] are a type of Shikigesha which appear in the [[Rikkokushi]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8610|access-date=2023-04-10|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
